stem: Find the mean of the dataset: $3, \ 7, \ 5, \ 9, \ 6$.
hint: Add all the values, then divide by how many values there are.
explanation: Sum $= 3 + 7 + 5 + 9 + 6 = 30$. There are $5$ values, so the mean $= 30 \div 5 = 6$.

stem: Find the mean of the dataset: $12, \ 15, \ 11, \ 14, \ 13$.
hint: Add all the values, then divide by how many values there are.
explanation: Sum $= 12 + 15 + 11 + 14 + 13 = 65$. There are $5$ values, so the mean $= 65 \div 5 = 13$.

stem: Find the mean of the dataset: $4, \ 8, \ 6, \ 2$.
hint: Add all the values, then divide by how many values there are.
explanation: Sum $= 4 + 8 + 6 + 2 = 20$. There are $4$ values, so the mean $= 20 \div 4 = 5$.

stem: Find the mean of the dataset: $8, \ 10, \ 12, \ 14, \ 16$.
hint: Add all the values, then divide by how many values there are.
explanation: Sum $= 8 + 10 + 12 + 14 + 16 = 60$. There are $5$ values, so the mean $= 60 \div 5 = 12$.

stem: Find the median of the dataset: $2, \ 9, \ 5, \ 7, \ 3$.
hint: Order the values from smallest to largest, then find the middle value.
explanation: Ordered dataset: $2, \ 3, \ 5, \ 7, \ 9$. There are $5$ values, so the median is the $3$rd value: $\text{median} = 5$.

stem: Find the median of the dataset: $4, \ 8, \ 2, \ 10, \ 6, \ 14$.
hint: Order the values. With an even number of values, the median is the mean of the two middle values.
explanation: Ordered dataset: $2, \ 4, \ 6, \ 8, \ 10, \ 14$. There are $6$ values, so the median is the mean of the $3$rd and $4$th values: $\text{median} = \dfrac{6 + 8}{2} = 7$.

stem: Find the mode of the dataset: $3, \ 5, \ 3, \ 7, \ 9, \ 5, \ 3, \ 1$.
hint: The mode is the value that appears most often. Count how many times each value appears.
explanation: Tallying the values: $1$ appears once, $3$ appears $3$ times, $5$ appears twice, $7$ appears once, $9$ appears once. The value that appears most often is $3$, so $\text{mode} = 3$.

stem: Find the median of the dataset: $15, \ 9, \ 22, \ 18, \ 31, \ 22, \ 15, \ 22$.
hint: Order all $8$ values from smallest to largest, then average the two middle values.
explanation: Ordered dataset: $9, \ 15, \ 15, \ 18, \ 22, \ 22, \ 22, \ 31$. There are $8$ values, so the median is the mean of the $4$th and $5$th values: $\text{median} = \dfrac{18 + 22}{2} = 20$.

stem:
The frequency table below shows the number of pets owned by students in a class.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|}\hline \text{Number of pets} & \text{Frequency} \\ \hline 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 3 \\ 4 & 1 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Find the mean number of pets. Give your answer to one decimal place.
hint: Multiply each value by its frequency to find the total, then divide by the total frequency.

stem:
The frequency table below shows the scores students received on a quiz.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|}\hline \text{Score} & \text{Frequency} \\ \hline 4 & 3 \\ 6 & 4 \\ 8 & 3 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Find the mean score.
hint: Multiply each score by its frequency, add the results, then divide by the total frequency.

stem: Nine students score between $60$ and $80$ on a test, but one student scores $5$. Which measure of centre best represents the typical score?
options: Mean, because it uses every value in the dataset
Median, because it is not affected by the very low outlier
Mode, because it is the most common score
Range, because it shows the spread of the data
answer: Median, because it is not affected by the very low outlier
hint: Think about how the score of $5$ would affect the mean compared to the median.
explanation: The score of $5$ is an outlier that would drag the mean well below the $60$–$80$ range of the other nine students, making it an inaccurate representation of the typical score. The median is not affected by extreme values, so it better represents the centre of this dataset.

stem:
The frequency table below shows the number of goals scored per game by a football team over a season.

$$\begin{array}{|c|c|}\hline \text{Goals} & \text{Frequency} \\ \hline 0 & 3 \\ 1 & 5 \\ 2 & 6 \\ 3 & 4 \\ 4 & 2 \\ \hline \end{array}$$

Find the mean number of goals per game. Give your answer to one decimal place.
hint: Multiply each number of goals by its frequency, sum the results, then divide by the total number of games.
